fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The inductive hypothesis in an inductive proof is the assumption made in the inductive case that a property P we wish to prove holds for E already holds for some component or subordinate case that E depends on.

For example, E may be an arithmetic sum while a component might be a term t_i in E. We then seek to show that if P holds for every t_i in E, then it also holds for E. Together with a base case, we prove that P holds for E.

fr:title.text: Preservation of denotation under small-step semantics of the addition language

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Preservation of denotation under small-step semantics of the addition language

fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
A transition according to the small-step semantics of the language does not change the denotation:
\frac {e \rightarrow e'}{\llbracket e \rrbracket = \llbracket e' \rrbracket }

This property can be proved using structural induction on the structure of the expression e:
Base case
e = \text {Val}\ n
The base case is trivially true; it does not match any rule in the small-step semantics, because it is already in a terminal state and so there is no possible transition.
Inductive case
e = \text {Add}\ x\ y
If the precondition e \rightarrow e' has the form
\text {Add}\ (\text {Val}\ n) (\text {Val}\ m) \rightarrow \text {Val}\ (n+m)
then the conclusion
\llbracket \text {Add}\ (\text {Val}\ n) (\text {Val}\ m) \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Val}\ (n+m) \rrbracket
is true, because both sides evaluate to n+m.
If the precondition e \rightarrow e' has the form
\text {Add}\ x\ y \rightarrow \text {Add}\ x'\ y
for some transition x \rightarrow x', then we must show that
\llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Add}\ x'\ y \rrbracket
is true. \llbracket x \rrbracket = \llbracket x' \rrbracket is true by induction—we assume the preservation property holds as our inductive hypothesis—and \llbracket y \rrbracket = \llbracket y' \rrbracket is true by reflexivity. Under compositionality, we conclude that \llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Add}\ x'\ y \rrbracket .
If the precondition e \rightarrow e' has the form
\text {Add}\ x\ y \rightarrow \text {Add}\ x\ y'
for some transition y \rightarrow y', then we must show that
\llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y' \rrbracket
is true. The proof is the same as above. \llbracket x \rrbracket = \llbracket x \rrbracket is true by reflexivity and \llbracket y \rrbracket = \llbracket y' \rrbracket is true by induction. Therefore, \llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y' \rrbracket .

Structural induction is a syntax-directed proof style that defines a base case and an inductive case. The base case provides a term for which a property P holds, while the inductive case shows how—assuming P holds for some arbitrary subterm(s)—it also holds for a composition that includes these subterms.

Consider the case where you have an small addition language. Suppose we wish to prove that the denotational semantics of the language is total, that is, that that for every expression e there is an integer n such that \llbracket e \rrbracket = n. If we assume compositionality, we can prove totality holds using structural induction.

fr:title.text: The compositionality property of the addition language

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The compositionality property of the addition language

fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
The compositionality of the language may be defined as follows:
\frac {\llbracket x \rrbracket = \llbracket x' \rrbracket \ \llbracket y \rrbracket = \llbracket y' \rrbracket } {\llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Add}\ x'\ y' \rrbracket }
The base case is the constructor e = \text {Val}\ n.
This case is trivial given the semantics, specifically \llbracket \text {Val}\ n \rrbracket = n.
The inductive case is the constructor e = \text {Add}\ x\ y.

Assume \llbracket x \rrbracket = n and \llbracket y \rrbracket = m for some integers n and m. It follows from the valuation function that
\llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket x \rrbracket + \llbracket y \rrbracket = n + m
By assuming that the subterms of \text {Add} already satisfy totality—the so-called inductive hypothesis—we can prove that totality also holds for \text {Add}\ x\ y.
